coredata.dc:description: Due to their fascinating chemical, optical, electrical, and biological properties carbon dots (CDs or CDots), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted attention in biosensing as they can greatly improve the detection limit, sensitivity, and selectivity of biosensors. In general, CDs, CQDs, and GQDs are a class of carbon-based nanomaterials that are characterized by extraordinary fluorescence, a size less than 10 nm, high stability, low toxicity, and being easy to synthesize and presenting functional groups in their surface area that vary according to their synthesis source. In this review, a general description of the main methods and precursors reported in the scientific literature for the synthesis of CDs, CQDs, and GQDs are presented, as well as the chemical, optical, electrical, and biological properties that stand out the most from them; moreover, the main objective of this review is to summarize the application of these carbonaceous nanomaterials in biosensors for the detection of communicable and non-communicable diseases. The article summarizes the applications of CDs, CQDs, and GQDs according to the group of diseases they detected using the international classification of diseases in its 10th edition (ICD-10). To facilitate the reader's access to significant information from these biosensors, several tables summarize the information associated with the type of biomarker, the working ranges, and the biosensor assembly.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to their fascinating chemical, optical, electrical, and biological properties carbon dots (CDs or CDots), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted attention in biosensing as they can greatly improve the detection limit, sensitivity, and selectivity of biosensors. In general, CDs, CQDs, and GQDs are a class of carbon-based nanomaterials that are characterized by extraordinary fluorescence, a size less than 10 nm, high stability, low toxicity, and being easy to synthesize and presenting functional groups in their surface area that vary according to their synthesis source. In this review, a general description of the main methods and precursors reported in the scientific literature for the synthesis of CDs, CQDs, and GQDs are presented, as well as the chemical, optical, electrical, and biological properties that stand out the most from them; moreover, the main objective of this review is to summarize the application of these carbonaceous nanomaterials in biosensors for the detection of communicable and non-communicable diseases. The article summarizes the applications of CDs, CQDs, and GQDs according to the group of diseases they detected using the international classification of diseases in its 10th edition (ICD-10). To facilitate the reader's access to significant information from these biosensors, several tables summarize the information associated with the type of biomarker, the working ranges, and the biosensor assembly.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The encyclopedia Britannica defines disease as “any harmful deviation from the normal structural or functional state of an organism, generally associated with certain signs and symptoms and differing in nature from physical injury” [1]. Depending on the ability of a disease to spread from one person to another, diseases can be classified as chronic non-communicable (CNCD) (only affect the organism exposed to the disease) or communicable (CD) (the infecting agent can spread directly or indirectly from the host to other organisms). Non-communicable diseases are health alterations different from injuries, maternal and perinatal conditions, and nutritional deficiencies that are not transmissible from person to person [2] and are generally produced by a combination of genetic, physiological, environmental, and behavioral factors [3].

According to the World Health Organization (WHO), between 2000 and 2019, the mortality and morbidity profiles for CD had a worldwide decline increasing the global share of CNCD deaths from 60.8% to 73.6%. While CNCD accounted for up to over 85% of deaths in high-income countries, the CD (including lower respiratory infections, diarrheal diseases, malaria, tuberculosis, and HIV/AIDS are part of the top 10 causes of death) along with maternal, perinatal and nutritional conditions were still responsible for nearly half of all deaths in low-income countries [4].

Emerging only at the end of 2019, the COVID-19 pandemic affected 153 million people, causing more than 3.2 million death by May 1, 2021 globally; which made it the main cause of death, causing indirect deaths and shortened life expectancy in many countries, with still unknown long-term impact on morbidity [4]. By 2030 non-communicable diseases could cost the world economy around 47 trillion dollars [2], while the cost of large-level pandemics such as Covid-19 and moderate pandemics generated by other communicable diseases is approximately 3.5 trillion dollars and 570 billion dollars [5], respectively.

Since the number of premature deaths associated with CNCDs and CDS can be reduced by improving prevention and early diagnosis, most of the global efforts are associated with novel detection and prevention methods and technologies [4]. Detection has proven to be an essential part of the entire health system, as it is involved in early diagnosis and treatment selection [6]. Biosensors are some of the most recent developments for rapid and sensitive diagnostic tests for CNCDs and CDS. Biosensors are devices that detect changes in biological signals (biomarkers) and transform them into quantifiable signals with a transducer [7]. Biomarkers are specific biomolecules associated with each disease and can be chemical compounds, proteins, carbohydrates, lipids, or nucleic acids [8].

In recent years, the development of biosensors has included different types of materials and nanomaterials and one of the most attractive nanomaterials has been carbon dots (CDots or CDs), and carbon quantum dots (CQDots), and graphene quantum dots (CGDots). Biosensors based on carbon dots technologies take advantage of the optical properties (fluorescence, turnability), size (particles smaller than 10 nm with quantum confinement properties), modifiable surface, and low toxicity [9,10]. In the past 10 years, carbon dots unique properties have allowed the development and publication of several types of biosensors including biosensors for CNCDs and CDS. Therefore, this review aimed to present the different types of biosensors based on CDots, CQDots and CGDots developed for CNCDs and CDS and summarize their most important properties and characteristics. In this review, biosensors based on CDots, CQDots, and CGDots were divided according to the group of diseases they detected using the international classification of diseases in its 10th edition (ICD-10) [11]. The type of biomarker, the working ranges, and biosensor assembly were extensively reviewed.

The nomenclature of “carbon dots” (CDots or CDs) is commonly used to describe several types of fluorescent carbonaceous nanomaterial, however, although they have similar properties, they are not the same. In this context, a lack of clarity has been detected in the concepts that involve their description [12]. In principle, these carbonaceous nanomaterials differ mainly by their geometry, their fluorescence mechanism, and their crystalline or amorphous structure, although most authors only distinguish them by their geometry [13]. From a geometric point of view, CDots, CQDots, and GQDots are 0-dimensional (0D) nanomaterials made up of carbon atoms in sp2 and/or sp3 hybridization. One major difference is that CDots and CQDots are quasi-spherical-carbon nanomaterials and the GQDots are small graphene sheets [14,15]. Therefore, the crystalline or amorphous structure of each nanomaterial is closely related to its fluorescence mechanism. First, CQDots and GQDots have a completely crystalline structure and their fluorescence mechanism is usually due to the quantum confinement effect or the surface state effect, on the contrary, CDots have an amorphous structure and their fluorescence mechanism is usually by molecular fluorescence or by the surface state effect [12,16,17].

The structure and morphology of these nanoparticles depend on each system because countless precursor and reaction method combinations are used to produce CDs, CQDs, or GQDs. However, because these nanoparticles consist of three main elements: carbon (C) in four different types of C=C/C–C, C–N, C–O, and C–N [18]; oxygen (O) [19]; and nitrogen (N) [20,21], they have a basic structure model compound for a core of mostly sp2 or sp3 carbon [22], and a surface or shell with groups of –COOH, –OH, and C=O (Fig. 1 ) [23,24]. Although the morphology of CDs and CQDs generally appears to be spherical in shape with an average diameter of 1–10 nm, the differences in size can be attributed to the different reaction conditions such as temperature [25]. There is undoubtedly a unique distribution of structures that are highly dependent on the synthesis process, and the deep structural characterization of these particles is currently lacking in the research literature, however, some authors [23] have demonstrated that the combination of data from RAMAN and electron spin resonance (ESR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), mass, titration, atomic force microscopy (AFM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) allows developing well-defined and feasible structural models to improve the applications of CDs, CQDs, and GQDs.

After the discovery of carbon dots (CDs, CDots), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), these materials have attracted significant interest from researchers around the world due to their intrinsic properties, such as low cytotoxicity, cell permeability, good aqueous dispersion, surface modification flexibility, high photo-stability, shorter acquisition time, high electrical conductivity, excitation-dependent multi-colored emission, and size no greater than 10 nm [26,27]. In recent years, medical, chemical, and industrial fields have been developing and innovating analytical methods based on CQDs, GQDs, and CDs thanks to the use of their optical, electrical, chemical, and biological properties [28]. These properties allow diverse applications such as diagnosis of diseases, biological imaging, creation of photovoltaic devices, and biosensors [26].

Over the last decade, different approaches for synthesizing carbon dots have been proposed; these can be grouped into two general categories: top-down and bottom-up. Top-down approaches are based on synthesizing carbon dots through the breakdown of larger carbon materials such as carbon nanotubes, graphite, and activated carbon, among others; to generate this breakdown the most used synthesis methods are laser ablation, chemical ablation, electrochemical, and ultrasonication [29]. On the other hand, bottom-up approaches are based on the synthesis of carbon dots through the transformation of carbon precursors into large structures by chemical or physical forces at the atomistic or microscopic level [30]. Some precursors used are citrate, carbohydrates, or small non-conjugated molecules, in addition, in bottom-up approaches, the most common synthesis methods are ultrasonication, hydrothermal/solvothermal, electrochemical, and microwave-assisted [31]. Table 1 (supplemental material) synthesizes the advantages and disadvantages of each synthesis method.

The laser ablation synthesis method has become key to the synthesis of nanoparticles with controlled geometry and size, is characterized because it is environmentally friendly and can be very fast and efficient since a large volume can be produced in a short period; but it must be taken into account that its operation is complicated and requires high energy consumption, which generates high costs [32–34]. This synthesis method is common in nanomaterials derived from carbon such as carbon dots [33,35]. Nanomaterials are produced by irradiating a carbon-based target with laser pulses at a specific wavelength for a certain time, producing the release of different carbon nanomaterials and the obtention of carbon dots after filtering the solution [34]. Some of the reported carbon materials are carbon dust [36], graphene [37], multi-walled carbon nanotubes [38], and flake graphite [39], among others.

It is worth mentioning that a UV laser source with a short wavelength and a short irradiation time produces CQDs smaller than 10 nm with similar size distribution and the highest emission intensity. On the contrary, a longer wavelength and extended times of irradiation results in agglomerations, which result in a broadening of the emission band [34].

The chemical oxidation synthesis method allows a large-scale production, and is based on the use of strong oxidizing acids to carbonize a carbon material and obtain CDs by carefully controlling the oxidation reaction conditions since this way can lead to smaller particle sizes; so, not controlling these reactions it usually results in a lack of homogeneity in the size distribution of the obtained particles [40]. Although this method uses strong oxidizing acids and requires difficult and drastic conditions and procedures that could be a disadvantage, it is important to highlight that it offers a variety of accessible materials as carbon sources and this is what is most striking [29]; some materials are activated carbon [41–43], carbohydrates [44], acetic acid [45], polyethyleneimine [46], among others.

The electrochemical synthesis method may differ in systems of two and three electrodes. Depending on the number of electrodes; these electrodes are called 1) working electrodes, 2) counter electrodes, and 3) reference electrodes [47]. To obtain carbon dots, the electrodes are placed in a solution that works as an electrolyte. Then the counter electrode supplies current to the working electrode, often producing oxidation or reduction [48], either from a precursor such as citrate sodium, urea [49], ethanolamine [50], ethanol [51], among others, or a precursor with a high carbon content such as graphite [52], graphite rods [53], carbon nanotubes [54], among others. It must be noted that CDs can be produced by the electrochemical synthesis method with a low cost, high yield, and controllable morphology and size, but the harsh circumstances required, and the complicated procedures restrict their use and could be a disadvantage of this method [47].

The ultrasonication synthesis method (also known as sonochemical) is a simple, rapid, and environmentally friendly way to obtain CDs [55], with a size distribution that can be easily controlled by adjusting the time, the frequency, and the power of the ultrasound [56,57]. According to Chemical Methods Ontology (CMO), ‘ultrasonication’, registered as CMO:0001708, refers to a method that is based on the irradiation of a liquid sample with waves of frequency greater than 20 kHz. These waves result in alternating cycles of low pressure, in which the high-intensity sonic waves create small vacuum bubbles or voids in the liquid, and cycles of high pressure, in which the created bubbles violently collapse creating very high local temperatures. When the irradiation is with waves of frequency between 20 Hz and 20 kHz, it is known as ‘sonication’. The standard sonochemical process for CDs is that the carbon precursors or materials, in a suitable solvent, are subjected to sonication for desired time [55,58]. One of the main challenges of this synthesis method is the unavailability of large-scale ultrasonic reactors, and the direct contact between the processing liquids, and the ultrasonic horns [57].

The hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis method involves the use of a solvent that is sealed in a container (reactor or autoclave) with a carbon precursor at high temperatures (>100 °C) and pressures (>100 kPa) to form crystalline materials [59,60]. The carbon precursor and solvent sealed at high temperature and pressure allow hydrolysis, dehydration, decarboxylation, polymerization, and aromatization reactions to occur [60]. If this synthesis method uses water as a solvent the method is called the “hydrothermal synthesis method”, but if it uses a nonaqueous solution with organic solvents it is called the “solvothermal synthesis method” [59,61]. In this method, the sealed container is used under subcritical or supercritical conditions of the solvent, and the hydrothermal synthesis method is usually performed below the supercritical temperature of water at 374 °C [59,62]. Also, this synthesis method is cheap and environment friendly, and can use waste with high carbon content as precursors to obtaining CDs with high purity, adaptable particle size, narrow size distribution, and controllable morphologies [59,63,64]. However, the hydrothermal method is also inherently risky, because it operates at high pressure and temperatures, and its use is limited by requiring expensive and complex reactors that consume a lot of energy [65]. In addition to waste, other carbon precursors can be used, such as citric acid and diethylenetriamine [66], powdered egg white and phosphoric acid [67], gelatin [68], grapefruit peel [69], glucose [70], α−D-Glucose 1-phosphate dipotassium salt hydrate, β-D-Glucosamine pentaacetate, D-gluconic acid and sucrose in DMSO-water [71], graphene oxide (GO) dissolved in DMF [72], among others.

Higher synthesis temperature led to higher quantum yield, smaller particle size, and narrower and more blue-shifted fluorescence emission, while a lower synthesis temperature led to a greater UV–visible absorbance and more red-shifted fluorescence emission [73]. In recent years, the long wavelength emitting CDs have been the focus because of their promising potential in cell imaging, in vivo imaging, labeling, and diagnosis [74].

Microwave-assisted synthesis method produces carbon dots in a simple, fast, and inexpensive process [75], using readily available precursors such as organic substances such as glucose [76], citric acid and diethylenetriamine [77], banana peel [78], and chitosan gel [79], among others. Also offers a different approach by directing the microwave irradiation towards the center of the container and penetrating directly into the material, resulting in rapid and uniform heating [75]. This happens because the solvent is heated at the molecular/atomic level because of relaxation mechanisms caused by the absorbed energy of the microwaves [80]. This method is widely used because the reactions are carried out much faster than with thermal heating, and the reduction in the reaction time is quite significant, being of the order of minutes. However, there are disadvantages such as that it is restricted to small volumes, which limits its application in large-scale reactions, in addition, the pressure of the system is also limited, which reduces the usability of solvents with lower boiling points [75,81,82].

Carbon dots are organic semiconductor nanoparticles (carbon as the predominant element) [83] of 0 dimensions (0D) (CQDots and CDots) or 2 dimensions (2D) (CGDots) formed by sp2, sp3 hybridized carbon atoms or a mixture of both carbon hybridizations [14]. They are composed of hundreds or thousands of atoms arranged in a crystalline structure of usually spherical shape and nanometric dimensions. In addition, they have a functionalized surface with groups that contain oxygen derived from precursors or generated during synthesis, causing significant aqueous dispersion [84]. In some cases, carbon dots production requires additional steps such as surface passivation, which improves their optical properties, functionalization to give them specific chemical reactivity [14], or doping with heteroatoms to improve their electrical and/or optical properties [85]. Furthermore, these processes are carried out with various organic, polymeric, inorganic, or biological materials that can modify their dispersion in aqueous and non-aqueous solvents [29,86], obtaining either hydrophilic carbon dots or hydrophobic carbon dots [84]. It should be noted that carrying out any of these additional steps just mentioned (functionalization, passivation, or doping with heteroatoms) is the key point to customizing the desired characteristics of carbon dots [87–90].

CDs ability to emit light depending on excitation has been the main object of study in nanomaterials. Luminescence is commonly classified by the origin of the energy supply required to cause the excitation of electrons. Some of the most common forms of luminescence in carbon dots are electroluminescence (EC), chemiluminescence (CL), bioluminescence, and photoluminescence (PL). Likewise, it is also common to classify photoluminescence by light emission time: short duration emissions are known as fluorescence (FL) and long duration emissions are known as phosphorescence [91,92].

Numerous studies have reported on the photoluminescence mechanism of carbon dots, however, the physicochemical fundamentals of light emission from carbon dots are still debated. Knowing the photoluminescence mechanism of carbon dots is important to adjust their optical properties and extend their applications in analytical chemistry [93]. Although there is not a unified explanation, the photoluminescence mechanism of the carbon dots is generally related to the quantum confinement effect [94–96], the surface state [96–98] and the molecular fluorescence [16,17,99]. The lack of agreement is because carbon dots can be obtained through different synthesis methods, precursors, and post-treatment methods, which translate into multiple variables. These methods affect their photoluminescence mechanism due to changes in carbon dots’ structures and surface [16]. However, regardless of the photoluminescence mechanism, carbon dots can emit ultraviolet (UV), visible or infrared (IR) radiation due to the radiative transition of electrons between an excited state and a lower state.

Carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon dots show great conductivity and are excellent electron acceptors and donors. Because of these, they are ideal for modifying electrode surfaces as they provide electron transfer pathways [84,100]. These pathways are dependent on the complex interaction between the carbon nucleus, the functional groups, and the doped heteroatoms. Additionally, carbon dots are promising in the functionalization of electrodes due to the increase in their active area [101]. heteroatoms doping is a very common form to control the electron transfer path of carbon dots. doping is a procedure that introduces additional atoms during the carbon dots synthesis such as nitrogen, boron, and sulfur, among others [102], allowing a significant change in electronic structure by modifying the forbidden band or energy gap. As result, carbon dots obtain properties to catalyze or inhibit redox reactions on their surface [103].

Carbon dots have gained meaningful attention in part because of their frontline biological properties, most notably their low cytotoxicity and plausible cell permeability. Their low cytotoxicity is attributed to their high carbon content and inert chemistry, and their good cell permeability is attributed to their small size (<10 nm) [104]. At low concentrations (Ex: 10–50 μg/mL) carbon dots have low cytotoxicity and are degraded or eliminated directly by the excretory system. On the contrary, increasing their concentration (Ex: 1 mg/mL) potentially increases their cytotoxicity [101]. In some cases, their cytotoxicity increases in the presence of an external stimulus such as light irradiation due to the production of reactive oxygen species. In these cases, some type of chemical modification of the surface is necessary to suppress the formation of these species [84]. Cell permeability is also critical for some applications such as imaging, drug administration, diagnosis, and tumor therapy [105], among others. For example, Qin et al. [106] investigated carbon dots (30 μg/mL) permeability without any type of functionalization in living cells and found they could penetrate the cell membrane and accumulate in a lysosome in 1 min, reaching a stable state after 10 min of incubation. Likewise, other authors such as W. Kong et al. [104] have discovered that by increasing the presence of the functional groups OH/COOH, PEG, and NH2 on the surface of carbon dots, their permeability in HeLa cells can be improved by 8%, 13%, and 19%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dot-based biosensors for disease detection
ce:section.ce:para: CDs gradually possessed outstanding properties for various applications because of their facile surface modification and preparation, biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, good stability against photobleaching, and high-water solubility that allows homogeneous sensing assays [101]. CDs have received a lot of attention in the development of biosensors due to their large specific surface area, high activity, extremely small size, and particularly their fluorescent characteristic, including high QY and long photoluminescence (PL) decay lifetimes [107–109]. These properties allow combined the high selectivity provided by specific bioreceptors and the high sensitivity of the fluorescence detection to develop sensitive, specific, fast, and low-cost biosensors [110].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nervous system diseases: Alzheimer's and Parkinson's
ce:section.ce:para: The nervous system oversees leading and coordinating all conscious and unconscious activities in the organism. All functions are divided between the central nervous system (CNS), made up of the brain and spinal cord, and the peripheral nervous system (PNS), composed of cranial nerves, spinal nerves, and peripheral ganglia [111]. Alzheimer's disease is a progressive and irreversible disorder that causes brain cells to degenerate and die, and deteriorates brain functions such as memory and thinking skills, interrupting the ability of a person to function independently [112]. Parkinson's disease is a disorder produced because of a dopamine (DA) deficiency, it is characterized by motor symptoms such as tremors at rest, rigidity, bradykinesia, and gait problems, among others, and by non-motor symptoms such as seborrhea, dementia, depression, sleep disturbance and more [113]. At this moment is no reliable quantitative diagnostic method for Alzheimer's disease or Parkinson's disease: the diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease is based on tests evaluating memory and cognitive abilities performed by a medical professional and assisted with some laboratory and imaging tests to detect dementia and determine precisely whether Alzheimer's disease or another condition is the cause [114]; in the case of Parkinson's disease diagnosis, it is eminently clinical based on the previously mentioned symptoms [115].

As detection method is scarce, several research groups have been developing biosensors that allow accurate and early detection of Alzheimer's and/or Parkinson's diseases [116,117]. The focus of this biosensors is the recognition of biomolecules such as proteins, ions, enzymes, neurotransmitters, carbohydrates, and DNA/RNA. This information is represented in Fig. 2 .

Catecholamines, especially dopamine (DA), and some of its precursors are the most used biomarkers to detect Parkinson's disease. DA is produced in neurons and is found in both the CNS and the PNS (Table 2 (supplemental material)). Within the brain, AD is mainly associated with neurons of the nigrostriatal pathway, which is prominently involved in the control of motor function; one of the main functions of this pathway is the stimulation of intentional movement, therefore, its degeneration leads to motor symptoms of Parkinson's disease [118]. Taking advantage of the luminescence characteristic of CDots, DA can be detected, allowing the use of electrochemical [119,120] and optical [121,122] biosensors. Biosensors for detecting DA are based on quantifying the increase or decrease of a signal due to the interaction between CQDs and DA or a molecule associated with the oxidation of DA. Some reported investigations on biosensors based on CQDs to detect and quantify DA in real samples are presented below, characterized by their low detection limit, high selectivity, and sensitivity.

Y. Jiang, B. Wang, F. Meng, Y. Cheng, and C. Zhu [119] propose an electrochemical biosensor based on a modified electrode with nitrogen-doped CDots (NCDots) and without any post-treatment for direct detection and rapid detection of DA in human serum and urine samples. Similarly, it is common for electrochemical biosensors to use CDots as a complementary material to improve the properties of other materials. The main materials combined with CDots are gold nanoparticles (AuNP) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO). AuNP-CDots are often used to increase sensitivity, electrocatalytic activity, and/or specific recognition of analytes [123–125]. Q. Huang et al. [124] reported that the use of AuNP-CDots modified with chitosan (CS) attached to glass carbon electrodes (GCE) improved the resolution of overlapping peaks in substances such as ascorbic acid, uric acid, and DA. PPS Kumar, S. Megarajan, GRK Reddy, and V. Anbazhagan [123] used Pectin-derived CDots to improve the stability of the AuNP-CPE sensor (carbon paste electrode) by reducing Au3+ to Au0, detecting the oxidation of DA through transfer. As with AuNPs, CDots have been used to enhance the properties of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) for the electrochemical detection of DA. J. Fang, Z. Xie et al. [126] manufactured a novel carbon fiber (CF) microelectrode combined with rGO with a considerable increase in electrocatalytic activity, surface area, and charge storage capacity, related to the amplification function of the signal. Similarly, S. Hu et al. [120] developed a DA biosensor using a GCE with rGO and CDots on its surface therefore the negatively charged carboxyl groups present in the CQDs bind to DA through electrostatic interactions increasing the intensity of the signal.

DA Optical biosensors are based on the qualitative or quantitative measurement of an optical signal and are normally based on fluorescence [121,127,128] or chemiluminescence [122,129]. For chemiluminescence, changes in luminescence are measured and in the case of fluorescence, changes in fluorescence intensity are measured. C. Zhao, Y. Jiao, J. Hua, J. Yang, and Y. Yang et al. [121] proposed that CQDots can detect DA using the ketone groups present in DA and dopamine-quinone (product of DA oxidation under alkaline conditions) since they can accept electrons from CQDs, which result in a decrease in fluorescence intensity. In contrast, J. S. Anjali Devi, A. H. Anulekshmi, S. Salini, R. S. Aparna, and S. George et al. [127] detected DA thanks to the increase in fluorescence intensity. These authors functionalized the CQDots with amino phenylboronic acid (APBA) providing an electron transfer pathway for the DA amino groups to the CQDots/APBA. This stabilized the structure and produced an electron-hole responsible for the fluorescence increase. Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is another option used by Y. He, C. Pan, H. Cao, M. Yue, L. Wang, and G. Liang et al. [128], in this research, CDots were used as energy donors, and the AuNPs as energy acceptors, then they have joined them non-covalently ensuring that they were close enough to each other so that the AuNP could receive the energy transferred by the excited CDs without any photon emission. Finally, they managed to detect DA because its presence returned the fluorescence emission of the CDots.

CDots chemiluminescence is also used to detect DA and/or to improve the chemiluminescent properties of already known systems. M. Amjadi, JL Manzoori, T. Hallaj, and MH Sorouraddin [129] discovered that CDots could substantially improve the weak chemiluminescence signal produced by the Cerium (IV)/Thiosulfate system. When Thiosulfate is oxidized by Cerium (IV), SO2* molecules are generated in the excited state that act as energy donors for CQDots, consequently, Cerium (IV) manages to oxidize Thiosulfate and CQDs simultaneously, producing chemiluminescence from Thiosulfate and excited CQDs. Then, they detected DA by its interaction with the Cerium (IV)/Thiosulfate/CQDs system since DA competes with CQDs and thiosulfate for Cerium (IV), causing a reduction in the chemiluminescence signal. Similarly, M. Amjadi, T. Hallaj, J. L. Manzoori, and T. Shahbazsaghir et al. [122] used silicon-doped CDots (SiCDs) to enhance the chemiluminescence reaction between hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and bicarbonate (HCO3 −), the chemiluminescence intensity was increased 70 times after the functionalization of the SiCDots with hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) since their micellar structures protected the excited state of SiCDots and reduced their non-radiative deactivation. Catecholamines such as dopamine, epinephrine, and norepinephrine were detected by the decrease in CL intensity of the SiCDots/CTAB/H2O2/HCO3 − system.

DA biosensors based on CDs have been improving their limit of detection and the detection range (Table 2(supplemental material)) with values between 0.0025 and 450 μM, however, these values are further away from the LOD of current clinical tests measuring dopamine. The current laboratory test (catecholamine blood test) measures catecholamines on picogram per milliliter (pg/mL) and the normal level of dopamine measured is below 30 pg/mL. The working range and LOD of the CDots biosensor for dopamine (μM) need to be reduced at least 2 or 3 levels of magnitude to be a competitive test in a clinical setting. The combination of different types of nanomaterials and the evolution of signal processing can help to decrease the LOD and the working range for future clinical applications. However. The current LOD and working range can be used to develop kits to measure DA for less regulated industries and research applications that require measurement tools with faster turnaround times.
•
Other neurotransmitters

Levodopa (L-DOPA), a DA precursor and the most widely prescribed drug for treating Parkinson's disease, produces many adverse reactions that disturb the patients' quality of life. H. Li et al. [130] reported a CDots-based sensor to detect L-DOPA by conjugating CDots derived from polyethylene glycol (PGE) and tyrosinase (TYR) to transform L-DOPA into levodopachrome, a compound with strong absorption at 490 nm that overlaps with the spectrum emission of the CDs and produces a reduction in the fluorescence intensity signal.

Similarly, Alzheimer's disease has been linked to the production of acetylcholine (ACh), a neurotransmitter associated with different functions in the central nervous system such as reward, alertness, and memory [131]. L. Wang, X. Chen, C. Liu, and W. Yang [132] built electrochemical acetylcholine (ACh) biosensor based on laminar double hydroxides (LDH) of Nickel–Aluminum (NiAl) (NiAlLDH) mixed with CDots. This biosensor exhibited higher electroconductivity and electrocatalytic performance for ACh oxidation.

Finally, S. Zhu et al. [133] developed an electrochemiluminescent (EC) sensor with logical gates based on biomolecular systems, using the enantiomer of glutamate (L-Glutamate). Recent research reports showed an increase in the levels of this amino acid in the cerebrospinal fluids of patients with neurological disorders, such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease. This sensor was based on conjugating the enzyme glutamate oxidase (GluOx) to nanocomposites conformed by silver nanoparticles (AgNP), CDots, and luminol (Lum) (AgNP/CDots/Lum) and immobilizing them on the surface of a GCE to amplify the signal of EC and achieve a sensor with a low detection limit, good stability, and outstanding specificity.

Glutathione, a peptide that is endogenously synthesized as reduced glutathione (GSH) from L-cysteine (Cys), L-glutamic acid, and glycine, has recently been associated with Alzheimer's disease as patients diagnosed with it have a significantly lower GSH level in hippocampal regions compared to healthy subjects. CDot-based biosensors for detecting GSH are fluorescence-based sensors. J. Hou et al. [134] designed a turn-on sensor to detect GSH based on the extinction of fluorescence caused by Hg2+ on CQDots and the restoration of fluorescence by the highly interaction specific between Hg2+ and GSH.

Similarly, Y. Huang et al. [42] developed turn-on and turn-off biosensors for GSH detection, the turn-on biosensor used Cu2+ to extinguish the CQDots-BPMA fluorescence, and then restore it by the interaction between Cu2+ and GSH. The shutdown biosensor used Ag + to improve the fluorescence of the CQDots-BPMA and then GSH was added for triggering competition for Ag+ between BPMA and GSH. The GSH binds to the silver ions returning the CQDots fluorescence to its original state. A visual representation of this biosensor can be seen in Fig. 3 .

Likewise, C. Yang, W. Deng, H. Liu, S. Ge, and M. Yan [135] and Q.-Y. Cai et al. [136] successfully manufactured an optical turn-on biosensor based on nanocomposites formed by manganese oxide (MnO2) and CDots. Their sensors were able to detect GSH using the fluorescence reduction produced by the FRET phenomenon between CDots and MnO2. The presence of GSH reduced MnO2 to Mn2+ ions, causing the CDots to be released and resulting in sufficient recovery of the fluorescence intensity. Unlike the strategies previously mentioned, W.-J. Niu, R.-H. Zhu, S. Cosnier, X.-J. Zhang and D. Shan [137] functionalized CDots with triethylammonium tetrafluoroborate (A2) and immobilized them on the surface of a glass carbon electrode (GCE) by electropolymerization. This biosensor used Fe (CN) 63−/4− and S2O82 - to accelerate the transfer of electrons from CQDots obtaining an ultrasensitive electrochemical glutathione (GSH) biosensor.

Among the most representative enzymatic markers for detecting Alzheimer's disease are acetylcholinesterase (AChE), an enzyme responsible for maintaining ACh levels by catalyzing its hydrolysis to choline, and glutathione reductase, an important enzyme that catalyzes the reduction of GSSG to GSH. Z. Qian, L. Chai, C. Tang, Y. Huang, J. Chen, and H. Feng [138] have used CQDots obtained from activated carbon to design a turn-on sensor capable to detect AChE. Its detection mechanism is based on an initial fluorescence quenching due to the strong interaction between Cu2+ and carboxyl groups of the CQDots followed by a fluorescence recovery due to a stronger affinity between Cu2+ and thiocholine a product from the enzymatic hydrolysis of ACh. Similarly, Ren, J. Wei, J. Ren, L. Qiang, F. Tang, and X. Meng [68] designed a quenching sensor from gelatin-derived CDots to detect AChE activity, using AChE and choline oxidase (ChOx) to transform ACh into betaine and H2O2 and iron ions (Fe2+) to transform H2O2 into OH- ions, which quenched the CDots fluorescence. X. Yan et al. [139] designed a turn-on sensor based on the combination of CGDots and AuNP to measure the activity of the enzyme glutathione reductase (GR). In this case, GSH interacts with the AuNPs and CGDots mixture generating a sufficient distance between CGDots and AuNP particles to cancel the FRET phenomenon and cause a recovery of the fluorescence signal.

The use of CDots-based biosensor for neurological proteins has the potential to be used as a method in a clinical setting as these sensors have a similar or better limit of detection that the current AChE and GSH clinical methods. The current testing methods have an optimal result range between 176 and 323 μg/mL for GSH and 8–18 U/mL for AChE. The GSH and AChE biosensors are based on CDots (Tables 2 and 3 (supplemental material)). working range is below the current test and their LOD is between 2 and 3 magnitude levels vs the optimal values measured for these two proteins. The application of these methods for clinical use requires further investment to reach high standards in the measurement process including qualification and validation. Additionally, the manufacturing process for these biosensors becomes more important as the quality of the products is significantly higher and the presence of impurities that can affect the final results needs to be remediated using more purification steps and the availability of manufacturing suites for this novel process will be required.

Ionic species are other common markers for neurological diseases. H. Jin, R. Gui, Y. Wang, and J. Sun [140] developed a biosensor based on CDots synthesized by a hydrothermal method with carrots as a carbon source to detect sulfide ions (S2−). The sensor conjugated CDots with polyethyleneimine (PEI) and Blue Nile Chloride (NB) to produce the CDots/PEI/NB nanocomposites. The presence or absence of S2− was evaluated by the reduction in the fluorescence of the CDots/PEI/NB/Cu2+ system in absence of S2−, and the increment of fluorescence when S2− was present, Cu2+ separates from the surface of the CDots to form stable species recovering the fluorescence of the nanoparticles. X. Wu et al. [141] proposed peroxynitrite (ONOO−), a type of nitrogen-reactive species produced by mitochondria during cellular respiration, as a biomarker for the detection of Alzheimer's. CDots from o-phenylenediamine were produced to create a surface rich in amino groups covalently conjugated with triphenylphosphonium (TPP). The CDots-TPP were directed to the mitochondria and successfully detected ONOO− as its presence reduces the CDots-TPP fluorescence. Low Fe3+ levels correlate with diseases such as Parkinson and Alzheimer and for this reason, G.J. Ru et al. [142] developed a turn-off sensor with NCDots using hydroxyethylethylenediaminotriacetic acid (HEDTA) as a carbon and nitrogen source and found that the fluorescence intensity of the NCDs decreased with the increase of the Fe3+ concentration.

Abnormal uric acid (UA) levels in plasma, serum, and urine are indicative of various diseases including neurological diseases. As uric acid can selectively and effectively quench CDots [143], C. Zhao et al. [144] designed a sensor based on fluorescent CDots using pork meat as a carbon source without any additional treatment, the sensor detected UA in human serum and urine samples. Similarly, S.J. Zhuo et al. [145] developed a fluorescence quenching method for UA detection based on silver-doped carbon quantum dots (Ag-CQDots) since this biosensor is focused on the Ag species that could bind with UA promoting the transfer of electrons and/or energy producing a reduction of the fluorescence intensity. On the contrary, J. Yang et al. [146] designed a turn-on sensor for the sensitive detection of UA, first, they turned off the fluorescence of CDots based on the FRET phenomenon with manganese oxide (MnO2) nano-sheets, then they recovered the fluorescence with the introduction of UA in the detection system due to the decomposition of MnO2 into Mn2+. Similarly, M. Amjadi et al. [147] designed a sensor with CDots doped with sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N) with good linearity and able to detect UA in human samples.

The multiple activities of the organs, cells, and tissues of the body are coordinated by the interaction of hormones, neurotransmitters, and cytokines, more commonly known as chemical messengers [148]. An endocrine, nutritional and metabolic disease is defined as a condition that inhibits or overexpresses any of the chemical messengers, not including neurotransmitters [11]. According to the WHO [149], there is an increase in the prevalence of these diseases, especially in diabetes mellitus with morbidity of 422 million people [150] and obesity [151] with morbidity of 650 million people, causing the death of 1.6 and 2.8 million people per year, respectively.

Most endocrine, nutritional, and metabolic diseases can be managed with proper diet and exercise [152]. However, it is crucial to be able to diagnose these diseases quickly and effectively, since in many cases early detection can help treat the disease and reduce unwanted side effects such as neurological problems [153], the appearance of other thyroid diseases [154], and even preventing death [155]. The biomolecules most used as analytes for the detection of these diseases are biomolecules (proteins, nucleic acids, lipids, and carbohydrates), cells or tissues, vitamins, ions, and chemical compounds. The proportions in which they are used can be found in Fig. 4 . Glucose, vitamin B, and ascorbic and uric acids are biomarkers associated with endocrine, nutritional, and metabolic diseases most used in biosensors.

Ascorbic acid (AA) or vitamin C and vitamin B were detected by three types of biosensors based on photoluminescence [156–165], electrochemiluminescence (EC) [125,166], and chemiluminescence [167] (Table 6 (supplemental material)). Chemiluminescence-based biosensor, Liu et al. [167] used nanosheets of MnO2 for quenching CDots, obtaining a high specificity in the detection of AA. The oxidation reaction between the AA and the nanosheets generated the loss of quenching only in AA presence. Congruently, photoluminescence-based biosensors also used fluorescence attenuation of CDots to detect their target analytes in a more sensitive and specific way. The quenching methods used can be seen in Table 7 (supplemental material). Many authors [158,160,161,163,164] used the vitamin properties of quenching to extinguish fluorescence through FRET, and the suppression of fluorescence to the effect of the internal filter (IFE).

Glucose [168–174] and glucose oxidase [175–178] are the main carbohydrates and enzymatic biomarkers used for the detection of endocrine, nutritional, and metabolic diseases. Most Glucose biosensors relied on photoluminescence for its detection as can be seen in Table 8 (supplemental material). All reported glucose and GO biosensors were tested in human samples showing very promising results in the detection of glucose with good specificity and sensitivity. These biosensors realized direct quenching and the fluorescence intensity decreased linearly with the addition of glucose or glucose oxidase, obtaining proper working ranges and detection limits as small as 0.04 μM [176].

The most used ionic and chemical biomarkers as biosensors are uric acid [144,147,179,180] glucosamine [181], and peroxynitrite anion [182], cadmium ions [183], and phosphate [184]. As shown in Table 9 (supplemental material), all biosensors were based on photoluminescence, synthesized by hydrothermal method, and analyzed in human serum. Table 10 (supplemental material) shows the chemical elements that were used to quench the fluorescence of the CDots. However, Kumar et al. [181], Simões et al. [182], Qin et al. [179], and Zhao et al. [144] used methods for the direct detection of their analytes of interest. They mainly consisted of decreasing the intensity fluorescence of CDots when they interact with the biomolecule linearly in the presence of a wide range of concentrations. In Fig. 5 , a visual representation of optical biosensors based on CDots for the detection of Ions and chemical compounds could be seen.

Although only Zhao et al. [144] and Amjadi et al. [147] used environmentally friendly production methods, it is important to note that all biosensors were a fast, cost-effective solution with high sensitivity in the detection of their respective analytes.

CDs sensor with applications for endocrine nutritional and metabolic diseases are principally based on detecting the increase or decrease of fluorescence after the exposure of the analyte to the CDs. The use of a similar detection method and a similar matrix (blood or urine) to extract the samples can facilitate the development of platform type of tests and devices that can use CDs technologies to detect different types of diseases and analytes using fluorescence detection which in the future can facilitate portability and personal medicine.

Neoplasms refer to abnormal growth of cells or tissues in the body. Neoplasms can be benign and malignant. Benign neoplasms produce abnormal cell proliferation that remains localized and do not invade adjacent tissues, whereas malignant neoplasms or cancer tend to grow rapidly, invade surrounding tissues, and spread to other parts of the body through a process called metastasis [185]. Malignant neoplasms or cancer have high morbidity and mortality worldwide. In 2018, the World Health Organization (WHO) reported 18.1 million cases of cancer around the world and 9.6 million deaths from this disease. These numbers are expected to double by 2040, with a considerable increase in low- and middle-income countries, where two-thirds of cases worldwide will be present [186]. The interventions defined by the WHO include primary prevention, early diagnosis, multimodal treatment and survival, and palliative care. Besides, The WHO has defined investment in early cancer diagnosis systems as a key and priority strategy, especially in cervical, breast, and colorectal cancer [186].

CDots-based biosensors offer a fast and cost-effective method for the detection of malignant neoplasms, which have the potential to be used without the need for infrastructure, equipment, and trained personnel. Therefore, they constitute an alternative for developing early diagnostic systems [187]. Biosensors with application in cancer, have been developed for the detection of different types of biomarkers such as chemical compounds, ions, biomolecules, and whole cells or tissues. These chemicals can be found quantitatively elevated in cancer patients and are produced by cancer cells or other cells in the body in response to the presence of a tumor [188,189] and can be detected in urine, and blood, tissue, or biological fluid samples [101].

Biomolecules specifically proteins [69,190,191] and nucleic acids [192–194], are the most widely used biomarkers in cancer detection (Fig. 6 ) due to their highly specific inter-biomolecular recognition mechanism. These molecules have a complementary structure that binds selectively and recognizes its complement between a set or a mixture of biomolecules [195]. Furthermore, these biomolecules are related to the development of highly sensitive techniques, since they can be easily detected with a small sample volume [196]. The detection of cells or tissues, chemical compounds, and ions are faster and cheaper than biomolecules, however, they have complications in the reaction between biomarker and bioreceptor leading to ambiguous results and unreliable responses [197].

Diagnostic biomarkers allow the early detection of diseases, determine the origin of the tumor, and classify the pathology in its different subtypes. On the other hand, prognostic biomarkers objectively evaluate the general outcome of the patient's disease, regardless of treatment. They are useful in the selection of patients for interventions, yet they do not predict a direct response to them. Predictive biomarkers assess the probability of risk in a specific treatment or the differential results of different interventions [198,199]. Most of the CDots-based biosensors are developed for the detection of diagnostic and prognostic biomarkers, however, the developed methodologies could be adapted to a larger number of biomarkers.

Functional and structural protein alterations, derived from variations in the genome may cause damage to normal physiological processes and lead to the appearance of cancer [200]. Proteins showing considerable changes in their level of expression could be used as biomarkers for neoplasms. Some enzymes used as cancer biomarkers of this pathology in the design of biosensors based on CDots are alkaline phosphatase (ALP) [41,43,201–205], acid phosphatase [206], α – L – fucosidase [207], tyrosinase [208], lysozyme [209], and β-glucuronidase (βG) [210]; other proteins are alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) [211], cancer antigen CA125 [190,212], carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) [213,214], prostate-specific antigen (PSA) [215], cytochrome-C (Cyt C) [216], desmin [217] and p53 [69].

CDots-based biosensors that detect enzymes mostly use their catalytic activities with substrates such as p-nitrophenylphosphate (PNPP) or 4-Nitrophenyl-β-D-glucuronide (PNPG) which are used to obtain p-nitrophenol (PNP) [201,202,210], ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) that hydrolyzes and generates ascorbic acid [203–206], and adenosine triphosphate (ATP) that hydrolyzes into pyrophosphate anions (PPi) [43], and these are then hydrolyzed into phosphate ions [41]. Other biosensor systems are based on the intermolecular recognition of aptamers [209] and antibodies [208] with the enzyme biomarker of interest.

Biosensors that use PNPP as substrate relies on the internal filter effect (IFE) for the detection process. These CDots are doped with nitrogen, boron, or cyclodextrin. Nitrogen doping is done to form amino groups on the surface that form a complex with PNP, through attractive electrostatic interactions [202]. The use of heteroatoms such as nitrogen and boron makes possible to adjust the position of the conduction/valence band of the carbon material, which modulates some properties, such as the fluorescence of CDots [218,219]. On the other hand, cyclodextrin is reported as a modulator to improve the catalytic activity of the nanomaterial, since they form complexes with hydrophobic compounds to form “pockets” in which the reaction between the enzyme and the substrate occurs. This system is widely used in IFE systems since the development of these sensors requires that the spectrum of the absorbing molecules overlap with the excitation or emission spectrum of the fluorescent units. In this case, the absorbance of the PNPP substrate does not affect the fluorescence spectrum of the CDots, however, the product (PNP) that is given by the hydrolysis of the substrate with the enzyme ALP or βG has an absorption spectrum that overlaps with the emission spectrum of the CDots, allowing the extinction of their fluorescence, and therefore the indirect detection of the enzymes [201,210,220]. Fig. 7 shows a representation of how these biosensors detect the enzymes.

Other widely used systems are those based on the extinction of the fluorescence of CDots using a static extinction mechanism using cations such as iron [203], copper [41], or cerium [43]; causing the oxidation of hydroxyl moieties of the particles through potassium permanganate (KMnO4) [205]; or using mechanisms based on Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) through nanocomposites synthesized from MnO2 [204,221], graphene oxide (OG) [209] and gold nanoparticles [207]. In turn-on systems, the fluorescence of CDots is recovered due to the interaction of the enzymes ALP or ACP with substrates such as ascorbic acid 2-phosphate [203–206] and ATP [43], generating compounds responsible for breaking the bonds of KMnO4 molecules and iron and cerium cations with fluorescent particles. Oppositely, turn-off systems use copper ions with high affinity and enzymatic substrates such as pyrophosphate. ALP hydrolyze pyrophosphate to phosphate ions, which liberates copper ions to interact with the carboxylic terminals of CDots (this system can be used for the quantification of the ALP enzyme and pyrophosphate anions, which are biological indicators of pathologies such as cancer) [41]. The detection and quantification of tyrosinase used a photoinduced electron transfer system (PET) between the CDots as electron donors and the o-dopaquinone terminals as acceptor; o-dopaquinone is obtained from the conversion of conjugated dopamine to CDs through the enzyme tyrosinase [208].

Other turn-on and turn-off methodologies for the detection of enzymes do not use its catalytic activity, yet they are based on the specific recognition of aptamers and antibodies by enzymes. Wu et al. [209] developed a turn-on system in which aptamers were conjugated with CDots and interacted with graphene oxide (GO), the interactions between GO and the CDots produced fluorescence quenching. While, in the presence of lysozyme, the CDots/aptamers desorbed from the graphene oxide surface and interacted with the enzyme recovering the fluorescence. On the other hand, Keenan et al. [207] developed a turn-off system for the detection of α – L – fucosidase. In this assay α – L – fucosidase polyclonal antibodies (PAb) were conjugated to gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), and α-L-fucosidase monoclonal antibodies (MAb) were conjugated to CDots. Without the enzyme, the CDots do not interact with the AuNPs, so their fluorescence intensity is maximum, however, in the presence of α – L-fucosidase a sandwich-type assay is produced which brings the CDots closer to the AuNPs producing an extinction of their fluorescence.

CDots technology for detecting enzyme activities associated with cancer detection evidence how this technology can open the door to developing faster and more accurate methods. In this case, the use of CDots in cancer-related enzymes is an example of the potential of CDots as tools for measuring enzymatic activities. ALP is a traditional enzyme associated with different types of cancer and with well know enzymatic properties, however, CDots can be used as a platform to enhance the measurement of enzymatic activities that are associated with cancer or other diseases as CDots can enhance the fluorescence or allow the measurement of lower concentrations of enzyme. The capability of measuring low enzyme concentrations can be observed in the LOD of the CDots-based biosensors described in Table 11 of the supplementary material.
•
Non-catalytic proteins

Biosensors that detect non-catalytic protein biomarkers are based on electrochemiluminescence, photoelectrochemistry, and photoluminescence. Electrochemiluminescent biosensors are based on the conductive properties of immune complexes formed with CDots. Either through direct detection of the biomarker, which drastically decreases the response of the sensor [211] or through sandwich-type formats that seek to improve the electron transfer capacity of the device. In the sandwich format, antibody 1 (Ab1) is immobilized to the working electrode with silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), AuNPs, and/or poly (5-formylindole)/reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite (P5FIn/erGO). While CDots or CQDots are conjugated with antibody 2 (Ab2) and AuNPs or NH2 - mesoporous silica nanoparticles (NH2 - MSN), these immune complexes seek to facilitate ion transport during redox reactions and provide large surface areas for immobilization of biomolecules [190,213]. A visual representation of how these types of biosensors work can be seen in Fig. 8 .

Photoelectrochemical biosensors carry out the detection process in sandwich formats. They are based on the modulation of surface states by oxidation-reduction reactions and modulated photoelectrochemistry of CDots. Gong et al. [191] developed a split-type immunoassay in which the photocurrent is restored in the presence of the biomarker. For this, the Ab1s adhered to the well of the plate, while the Ab2s are conjugated with AuNPs and the enzyme ALP. CDots are conjugated with titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs) modified fluorine-doped indium tin oxide (FTO). In presence of KMnO4, photocurrent is inhibited by the oxidation of hydroxyl groups present on the surface of the CDots. Nevertheless, this photocurrent is restored by adding AAP, due to the regeneration of hydroxyl groups which occur in the presence of ascorbic acid. Shuzhen et al. [215] also developed an immunophotoelectrochemical system in which the photocurrent decreased in the presence of the biomarker, this is recognized by two molecules: monoclonal antibodies and aptamers conjugated with copper nanoclusters. Once the biomarker has been biorecognized, HNO3 is added to the medium, which breaks the interactions of the aptamer with the nanocluster, releasing Cu2+ ions that bind to the conjugate of CQDots and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), producing a decrease in the efficiency of the photocurrent passage.

Photoluminescence-based systems utilize the modulation of fluorescence that occurs between the bioreceptor and the CDots [214,216,217]. Miao et al. [214] and Ghayyem et al. [216] take advantage of the quenching that occurs between aptamers and CDots. CDots regain fluorescence in the presence of the respective biomarker which induces the breakdown of the π – π interactions between the fluorescent particles and the bioreceptor. Feng et al. [217] covalently conjugated CQDots to anti-desmin through bonds formed between –C(O)Cl and NH2, C(O)Cl groups formed by treating the –C(O)OH groups of CQDots with SOCl2. This covalent binding significantly improves and slightly shifts the maximum fluorescence emission peak, since post-passivated functional amine groups induce charge delocalization and adjust the working function of the carbon. Then, by adding the demine biomarker to the system, the fluorescence is extinguished. Other sandwich photoluminescence systems include the use of CDots as fluorescent markers for the detection system, which, once encapsulated with other nanomaterials such as silica nanoparticles (SiNP), notably improve fluorescence intensity [69]. The system developed by Hamd-Ghadareha et al. [212] is based on fluorescence quenching by FRET. It occurs once CDs conjugated with aptamers are brought closer to AuNPs conjugated with antibodies, during the biomarker recognition event.

The advancement of genetic and omic technologies has made it possible to obtain genetic and genomic information with clinical applicability in the management of pathologies such as cancer from the areas of diagnosis, prognosis, and prediction.

One of the first reported studies for the detection of DNA molecules was carried out by Bai et al. [222], in this study, CDots were extinguished using an electron transfer process with methylene blue molecules and recovered their fluorescence in the presence of ss-DNA and ds-DNA, however, under the same conditions, ds-DNA molecules caused greater fluorescence recovery than ss-DNA molecules because the bond between methylene blue and ds-DNA is stronger. More selective detection systems are based on the complementarity of the bioreceptor probe and the sequence of interest. The bioreceptor probe can be immobilized to the CDots surface using the amino ends of the DNA probe and to the CDots by glutaraldehyde [223], or the carbodiimide technique that immobilizes the probe covalently to the surface of CDots [224,225]. Mohammadi and Salimi [223] conjugated the DNA bioreceptor probe to CDots through glutaraldehyde and quenched their fluorescence using MnO2 nanosheets. While Qaddara and Salimi [224] conjugated through carbodiimide technique and used graphene oxide (GO) modified AuNPs to extinguish the fluorescence of the CDots. This method allowed them to obtain a detection limit of 15 fM and to discern with two base mismatched DNA. Zarei-Ghobadi et al. [225] developed a methodology for fluorescence extinction of CDots based on Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles, this system used a magnet that separates the NPs from the CDots, if the analyte of interest is present, it hybridizes with a part of the immobilized probe on the fluorescent particles, thus again preventing their interaction with the Fe3O4@AuNPs. If the analyte is not present, the CDots interact again with the NPs and their fluorescence is extinguished. Another turn-on system that does not immobilize the DNA bioreceptor probe on the surface of CDots is the one reported by Khakbaz and Mahani [192], in this article the bioreceptor probe is conjugated to the fluorescent molecule FAM, which is adsorbed to CDots by π – π interactions and quenches their fluorescence by FRET.

Cu2+ ions extinguish the fluorescence of CDots by a charge transfer mechanism. It has been used in the development of turn-on [226] and turn-off [227] detection systems. Pang et al. [226] reported a biosensor for the quantification of guanine, which is based on the coordination of these molecules with copper (II) ions; therefore, in the presence of guanine, Cu2+ is bidentate with guanine N7 and O6 [228,229], resulting in the desorption of copper ions on the surface of the CDs and their respective fluorescence recovery. On the other hand, Singh et al. [227] developed a system for the detection of apurinic/apyrimidinic (AP) sites in DNA. In the presence of ascorbic acid and ds-DNA, Cu (II) ions are reduced to Cu(0)/Cu(I), enabling the formation of copper Nanoclusters (NCs). However, if ds-DNA contains AP sites, the formation of NCs does not occur, so the Cu (II) remains free in the solution to bind to the CDots and extinguish their fluorescence.

Other methodologies detect the fluorescence of molecules such as cy3 [193], thiazole orange (TO) [230], and 5,7-dinitro-2-sulfo-acridone (DSA) [231]. This allows the development of systems that recognize various analytes and the expansion of radiometric systems. Zhong et al. [193] developed a biosensor for the simultaneous detection of BRCA1 RNA/DNA and TK1 RNA/DNA. This sensor is based on the interaction of conjugated CDots with a DNA probe that has the BRCA1 biomarker sequence; hairpin structures conjugated to the cy3 fluorophore and the TK1 biomarker sequence; and AuNPs conjugated with the complementary probes of the BRCA1 and TK1 biomarkers. The absence or presence of each biomarker is detected through simultaneous or intercalated emission or extinction of CDots and cy3. The systems reported by Yaokun et al. [231] and Jin et al. [230] are based on fluorescent radiometry that occurs between DSA and TO with CDots. These sensors were developed for the detection of microRNA with exosomal content, and ds-DNA and G-Quadruplex, respectively.

Z. Heger et al. [232], ] successfully constructed a stratospheric probe using 3D printing technology and used it effectively as a biosensor based on complexes of CQDots stabilized with polyethylene glycol (PEG) and conjugated with genomic DNA for the determination of DNA damage in near space conditions. This group assumes that oxygen atom electron pairs of CQDots can bind to DNA by hydrogen bonding under the effect of UV light and rapidly increase the fluorescence of the sample, thus increasing the fluorescent signal of the conjugate PEG-CQDots-DNA after exposure to UV rays can be considered as a marker of the level of DNA damage. Additionally, during their first flight, they sent all the data online to the PC unit on the ground, demonstrating that their stratospheric probe is robust enough to withstand an unfavorable environment, as well as efficient, accurate, and sensitive in detecting DNA damage.

CDots biosensors for Nucleic acid utilized the properties and characteristics in an integral form. DNA and RNA biosensors utilize the whole structure of the CDots to interact modify and produce a signal. The approach utilized for nucleic acids utilize can be established as a platform for different types of nucleic acid probes, and it can be mimicked by other types of diseases or target compounds to employ CDots in a more integral form.

As an alternative for proteins and nucleic acids, novel biosensors are being developed for the detection of small molecules (metabolites) that participate in the processes of cell signaling and homeostasis. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has been recognized as a cancer biomarker because cancer cells increase H2O2 production leading to accelerated senescence, cell damage, and inflammation by providing an appropriate microenvironment for the development of this pathology [233,234].

Most of the systems developed for H2O2 detection use electrochemical transducers, since they take advantage of the oxidative capacity and the greater electrocatalytic activity of CDots. CDots combined with other nanomaterials such as platinum nanoparticles (PtNPs), ionic liquid functionalized graphene oxide (IL-OG) [235], and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) [236], and AuPd alloy nanoparticles (AuPd-ANPs) [233] improve the performance of the device. Chen et al. [235] and Bai et al. [236] showed that modified glassy carbon electrodes (GCE) with nanohybrids improved their conductivity compared with the electrode without modification. Nanohybrids facilitated the transfer of electrons between the interface of the electrode and the H2O2 molecules and were able to provide high electrical conductivity and very good electrocatalytic activity. Xu et al. [233] also improved the electrocatalytic activity of carbon fiber microelectrodes utilizing the combined nanoenzymatic activity of AuPd-ANPs and graphene CDots, and the structural support of graphene quantum dots to facilitate the nucleation and growth of dispersed and highly charged AuPd-ANPs. Su et al. [237] took advantage of the electrocatalytic activity of CDots and their functional groups for the immobilization of horseradish peroxidase enzyme (HRP) on a GCE. This electrode combined with Nafion formed a system that enhances electron transfer and maintains enzyme activity.

Bhunia et al. [238] developed a turn-on H2O2 detection device based on CDots. The quenching was created by the π – π interactions between the heme units of hemoglobin and the aromatic rings of carbon quantum dots, while the fluorescence recovery was produced by the presence of hydrogen peroxide molecules. Chu et al. [239] developed a CDots-based turn-off system with multicolored photoluminescence properties. The biosensor produced three emission peaks using the same excitation wavelength. H2O2 was detected using the maximum emission wavelength in the red spectrum. The increment in H2O2 concentration produced a decrease in CDots and the shift of the maximum emission wavelength to longer wavelengths. This characteristic allows the development of biosensors independent of fluctuations in the optical power of the system.

The detection of cellular receptors reduces the time and cost of the technique since sample processing is not necessary in most cases. In addition, the visual detection of cancer cells using CDots allows the development of cytosensors that contribute to the investigation, diagnosis, and theragnostic of the pathology. Cytosensors obtain and evaluate functional information on the physiology and metabolism of biological processes that influence the behavior of tumors [240,241]. However, the development of these cytosensors can be challenging because the analyte of interest has a larger surface area than molecular analytes, and cells display several molecules on their surface that can interact non-specifically with the bioreceptor affecting the selectivity and/or sensitivity of the device [242,243].

CDots have low toxicity and high biocompatibility therefore, most CDots-based cytosensors utilize the internalization of CDots by cancer cells for their subsequent detection using bioimaging or the combination of fluorescence spectroscopy with confocal laser scanning microscopy. Soleymani et al. [244], Zhao et al. [241], and Jiao et al. [245] developed cytosensors for the detection of the folate receptor (FR) in cancer cells (HepG-2, MCF-7, MKN-45, HT-29), conjugating folic acid and CDots/N-CGDs. Duan et al. [246] and Guan et al. [247], developed systems for recognizing liver cancer HepG-2 cells. The CDots developed by Duan et al. [246] were internalized in the cells after 3 min and exhibited a higher fluorescence in the nucleus than in the cytoplasm. The protonation caused by the CDots disintegrated the endosomes leaving the Cdots free to form agglomerations in the nucleus. However, when the CDots were put in contact with healthy cells, they were not able to cross the nuclear pore complex and stain the cytoplasm and the cell membrane. Guan et al. [247] encapsulated CDots in liposomes increasing their quantum yield due to surface passivation. This method selective monitored and labeled cancer cells through the specific interaction between the D-mannose conjugated to liposomes and the mannose receptor (MR) present on the surface of these cells. Likewise, Zheng et al. [248] synthesized CDots from L-aspartic acid with the ability to penetrate the blood-brain barrier and interact specifically with C6 glioma cells without the need for a biomolecule that behaves as a bioreceptor. This system was dependent on the source of the CDots.

Gao et al. [240] developed a theragnostic system based on the release of doxorubicin (Dox) in cancer cells and the subsequent recovery of fluorescence by CDots. These fluorescent nanoparticles were conjugated, through electrostatic interactions, with polyethyleneimine (PE) and a probe with hyaluronic acid (HA) and the drug Dox. The latter extinguishes the fluorescence of CDots using the FRET mechanism. The HA bioreceptor directs the conjugated nanoparticle to cancer cell receptors. Inside the cell, the enzymatic digestion of HA results in the release of Dox and the recovery of fluorescence from the CDots modified with PE.

Although the methodologies that use CDots fluorescence for detecting cancer cells are widely extended, there are other methodologies based on electrochemiluminescence that not only allow the detection and quantification of cancer biomarkers but also study the effect of chemotherapeutic drugs. Wu et al. [249] developed a portable and disposable paper-based cytosensor for in-situ detection of anticancer drugs and cancer cells recognition. The authors conjugated an aptamer that recognizes MCF-7 cells to the Au@Pd nanoparticles on the surface of the paper electrode, then particles with PtNi alloy were loaded with CDots acting as the ECL label, which was conjugated to concanavalin A (Con A) that recognizes mannose on the surface of these cells.

Cancer detection using CDots bioimaging is an application that can facilitate a more dynamic study of cancer cells as several of the CDots sensors are on and off type. Combining modern microscopy techniques and CDots biosensors can produce a deeper understanding of metabolic or physiological modifications in the cancer cell and cancer tissues. CDots low toxicity and specificity will facilitate the study of simultaneous tissues and how cancer drugs can affect them.

A zoonotic disease is pathology or infection that can be transmitted naturally from animals to humans or vice versa. It is estimated that approximately 60% of human pathogens have a zoonotic origin, and factors such as urbanization, climate change, tourism, biological vectors, wildlife trafficking, and other anthropogenic and natural components influence the distribution and zoonotic patterns. These pathologies represent a threat to health, safety, and economic growth, therefore, developing study, prevention, detection, and treatment strategies is a priority in world public health [250,251].

Anthrax is a zoonotic disease caused by bacteria and it is transmitted by Bacillus anthracis, a Gram-positive bacterium found on all continents, causing approximately 20,000 to 100,000 cases annually throughout the world. There are three routes of exposure to this pathogen: i) respiratory exposure through inhalation of spores, ii) skin exposure, and iii) gastrointestinal exposure. Most cases due to skin and gastrointestinal exposure are produced by the handling and slaughter of infected cattle, or by the consumption of contaminated meat [252,253].

Since dipicolinic acid (DPA) is one of the main components of Bacillus anthracis spores, it has been adopted as the most common biomarker to identify anthrax disease [254]. Luminescent biosensors based on lanthanide ions such as Terbium and Europium are the most widely used method for the detection of DPA due to their sensitivity, narrow emission bands, and mainly, due to the affinity of these ions to the analyte of interest [255,256]. However, these measurements can be altered by the stability limitations of lanthanides under humidity and UV light conditions, and by intensity fluctuations arising from environmental or instrumental factors [257,258]. Therefore, the development of radiometric devices based on CDots and lanthanides for the detection of DPA has been chosen. Many of the reported studies [26,259–261] use the CDots signal as a reference, while the change in the fluorescent signal that occurs due to the interaction between lanthanide ions and the biomarker is used for detection and subsequent quantification of DPA.

Li et al. [262] developed a radiometric biosensor for the detection of DPA without the use of lanthanide ions. For this, CDots were synthesized showing two emission peaks (352 and 420 nm) at the same excitation wavelength (315 nm). The peak at 420 nm is considered the maximum peak of fluorescence intensity, while the peak with less intensity at 315 nm corresponds to an enhanced scattering light. The dispersion peak was used as the reference signal, while the peak at 420 nm was modified by the presence of Cu2+ ions and the analyte of interest. The emission signal decreased by the interaction with the ions and was recovered in the presence of DPA molecules. Fig. 9 shows a visual representation of how the biosensor detection method works.

An exciting opportunity for using CDots biosensors as a tool to fight bioterrorism is the development of fast and portable devices able to detect the presence of Bacillus anthracis in shipments mail or airports. CDots biosensors coupled with fast and easy detection systems such as fluorescent test papers, colorimetric tests, or fluorescence tests can be in the future an alternative to detect Bacillus anthracis in different matrices.

This section reviews and summarizes the use of CDots for the detection of different types of biomarkers related to communicable diseases. According to the international classification of diseases, communicable diseases are ‘some infectious and parasitic diseases and are defined as ‘diseases generally recognized as contagious or communicable’ [263].

S. Li, J. Wang et al. [264] reported a biosensor based on a fluorescence off-and-on lateral flow immunochromatographic assay using FRET detection. The biosensor included CDots conjugated with ovalbumin (OVA) as a donor signal probe and AgNPs conjugated with a monoclonal antibody as an acceptor signal for the detection of zearalenone. D. Bhattacharyya, P.K. Sarswat, M.L. Free [265] reported preliminary results of a biosensor for detecting volatile organic tuberculosis biomarkers (VOBs) using CDots and reported that VOBs reduced the CDots fluorescence.

CDots-based biosensors have been designed to detect pathogenic Escherichia coli (E. coli). Weng et al. [266] utilized a one-step dry-heating method to synthesize mannose-modified fluorescent CQDots (Man-CQDots) and successfully detected E. coli type wild 1. The detection was performed by selective binding between the CDots and the lectin unit FimH in the flagella obtaining a detection limit of 450 CFU/mL in a range of 103–108 CFU/mL. Similarly, X. Yang, L. Feng, X. Qin [267] detected the presence of E. coli O157: H7 through carboxyl functionalized graphene quantum dots (cf-CGDots) and conjugated to the antibody anti-E. coli O157: H7. This sensor had high specificity and a detection limit of 100 CFU/mL. S. Ahmadian-Fard-Fini, M. Salavati-Niasari, D. Ghanbari [268] detected E. coli presence by fluorescence reduction. CDots were synthesized using lemon extract or using citric acid as a carbon source, in this sensor, the CDots fluorescence was turned off by increasing the concentration of E. coli bacteria in a range of 0–9 × 107 CFU/mL.
