Similarly, CDots-based biosensors have been designed to detect Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa), a common pathogenic bacterium capable of infecting humans and causing severe lung disease. R. Gao, Z. Zhong, X. Gao, L. Jia [269] designed a sensor for the rapid and selective detection of P. aeruginosa through FRET phenomena between CGDots and 5-carboxyfluorescein (FAM). The biosensor is turned-off in the absence of the target bacteria as the fluorescence of FAM-cDNA is quenched by CGDots, and the fluorescence of FAM-cDNA is recovered by adding dDNA-P. aeruginosa since the complementary strand of DNA (cDNA) is more specific to the target strand of DNA (dDNA) than CGDots. Similarly, H. Wang et al. [270] developed a fluorescence assay to detect P. aeruginosa based on the FRET phenomenon. In this case between Cdots and GO. CDots were conjugated with highly specific DNA probes for P. aeruginosa and GO was added to extinguish the fluorescence and complete the detection system. The system showed high specificity towards P. aeruginosa as the system did not react with other ubiquitous bacteria, besides the sensor had a wide detection range and low detection limit.

Electrochemistry offers a wide variety of DNA biosensors for communicable diseases with high sensitivity, simplicity, low cost, and portability. Q. Huang, X. Lin, J.J. Zhu, Q.X. Tong [78] synthesized CDots using banana peels as a carbon source and developed a Pd–Au-CD nanocomposite which was immobilized on GCE. An E. coli toxin (colitoxin) biosensor was completed by immobilizing a single-stranded DNA probe on the Pd–Au-CD modified GCE electrode. the DNA sensor was also successfully applied to detect colitoxin target DNA in human serum.

Other novel approaches to detect certain infectious and parasitic diseases have been developed. S. Nandi, M. Ritenberg, R. Jelinek [271] demonstrated that CDots obtained from L-tartaric acid, lauryl chloride, and D-glucose, possess long hydrocarbon chains. This characteristic facilitated the CDots attachment to four species of bacteria: E. coli, S. typhimurium, P. aeruginosa, and B. cereus. The CDots fluorescence is dependent on the bacterial species with different fluorescence intensities and emission wavelengths for each species. In Fig. 10 could be seen how a CDots with a long hydrocarbon chain binds to the bacteria.

L. Yang et al. [272] reported a biosensor based on hundreds of CDots encapsulated in breakable organosilicate nanocapsules (BONs) for the rapid detection of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). The encapsulated CDots (CDots-BONs) were conjugated with anti-S aureus antibodies to specifically recognize S. aureus and the pathogenic S. aureus cells and CDots-BONs were incubated for 60 min. After incubation, NaBH4 was added to break the encapsulation releasing the CDots and emitting fluorescence. S. Liang, L. Qi, R. Zhang, M. Jin, Z. Zhang [273] developed an on-and-off biosensor to detect double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) of HIV in synthetic samples and human serum samples. This sensor was a combination of CDots, cadmium tellurium quantum dots (CdTeQDs), mitoxantrone (MTX), and dsDNA: In the absence of dsDNA, MTX was used as a CdTeQDs fluorescence quencher and in the presence of dsDNA, MTX intercalated in DNA structure releasing the CdTeQDs and recovering the system's fluorescence.

N.K. Singh, B. Chakma, P. Jain, P. Goswami [274] designed a new and specific single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) aptamer for Plasmodium falciparum protein glutamate dehydrogenase (PfGDH) using a rational approach following the SELEX technique. ssDNA was modified with an amine (NssDNA) and covalently linked to CDots that had been synthesized from monosodium glutamate. the biosensor was able to detect PfGDH and has the potential to become a sensitive, selective, and rapid diagnostic method for malaria.

CDots biosensors for communicable diseases have the advantage over other methods in the detection speed and sensitivity. These two factors are significant to avoid the transmission of these diseases. CDots biosensors can reduce the detection time from hours or days for some diseases into minutes or even seconds; similarly, CDots have higher sensibility allowing the detection of biomarkers earlier than current detection methods. Communicable diseases are more frequent in developing countries; therefore, to make CDots biosensors more accessible to communities affected by communicable diseases in developing countries, it is necessary for the financial and administrative support of private and public institutions to rapidly advance research, development, and manufacturing of CDots biosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dot-based biosensors for COVID-19 detection
ce:section.ce:para: During the last year, CDots biosensor have been developed for detecting COVID-19. Li et al. [275] proposed the use of GQDots for the detection of SARS-CoV-2 through ultra-low-field NMR relaxometry. This method proposed the conjugation of the GQDots with the SARS-CoV-2; therefore, the magnetic relaxation switches (MRSw) can specifically recognize SARS-CoV-2. This method is used with the vial completely sealed reducing the exposure of people to the virus and without any pretreatment. This one-step method is evaluated with ultra-low-field nuclear magnetic resonance (ULF NMR). In addition to this, Sharma et al. [276] reported an approach using nanoparticles using giant magneto resonance (GMR) as a biosensor for the detection of SARS-CoV-2. In this method, the interactions between nanoparticles and non-magnetic viral proteins are responsible for changes in resistance and therefore in magnetization. These signals contain low levels of background noise and these GMR-based sensors can be transferred to portable devices. Likewise, the use of fluorescent biomarkers such as carbon dots is discussed to help isolate DNA fragments and minimize detection problems since they can act as signal enrichment tools [277]. Santiago et al. [278] proposed carbon dots as a tool to improve the specificity and sensitivity of the COVID-19 detection immunoassays. This method proposed carbon dots to amplify the antigen-antibody binding signal. These labels can be used either as a direct signal or be involved in additional steps to produce a proper signal. Similarly, Kotta et al. [277] propose various possibilities for the detection of COVID-19 such as detecting viral RNA through an ultra-sensitive biosensor using carbon dots and gold nanoparticles. They also propose the use of carboxylic CQDs for nucleic acid detection.

The advancement in the development of biosensors from CDots have shown to be a promising technology with methodologies that have significant advantages such as rapidness, sensitivity, specificity, accessible for populations at risk, easy to use, and robust in transport and storage. Additionally, they are designed to be able to be used near the patient through clinical samples such as urine, saliva, blood, or sweat. although the development of biosensors based on CDots for the detection of biomarkers in recent years is impressive, the commercial availability of these systems is still limited. Some of the reasons for the lack of commercial availability is the instability of the devices produced by the interference from other molecules found in the biological matrices analyzed, creating difficulties in assembling the biomolecules of the device on a large scale, and therefore, the reliability in the production methods. To achieve commercialization, it is necessary to better understand the origin of the fluorescence of CDots, their chemical composition, and their nanostructure, which hinders the reproducibility and repeatability in the CDots synthesis batches. Additionally, to develop better cost-effective devices, it is necessary to develop more devices with regenerable biological interfaces and biosensors with the ability to analyze multiple analytes. The global market for biosensors is growing at an accelerating rate and is expected to reach 50 billion USD in 2025, therefore, a strong and equitable synergy between academia, government, and industry is required to bring CDots biosensor systems from the laboratory to the market, and address the outstanding challenges and opportunities associated with CDots technologies.

Devised and directed the manuscript K·B; designed and conceptualized the paper K·B, J.P.A, M.S.M, J.P, M.E.L, and M.J; conducted the analysis K·B, J.P.A, M.S.M, J.P, J.P, S.L, C.M, S.T, S·B, M.E.L, and M.J.; wrote the manuscript in consultation, K·B, J.P.A, M.S.M, J.P, J.P, S.L, C.M, S.T, S·B, M.E.L, and M.J. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.
